entries.name: β-lactams (class — general overview)
entries.memo: They mimic **D-ala-D-ala** and block the **PBP transpeptidases** → fragile cell wall (bactericidal); resistance = **β-lactamases**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: rigid analogues of the D-alanine–D-alanine dimer; they fool the transpeptidases by substituting for the substrate and irreversibly block the formation of the cross-links (transpeptidation) of peptidoglycan → weak cell wall → lysis. Bactericidal.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: Penicillin Binding Proteins (PBP) with transpeptidase activity; the β-lactam ring (reactive due to the strain of the 4-membered ring) undergoes nucleophilic attack toward the catalytic serine (-OH) of the PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: spectrum/penetration depend on size (small molecules), lipophilicity and charge. Gram+ (often peptidoglycan → access to the periplasmic space); Gram- (phospholipidic outer membrane → porins required for small hydrophilic molecules). Specific parameters → see subclasses.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: variable per molecule → see subclass card.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: hypersensitivity → see individual classes.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: 2 main mechanisms — (1) β-lactamases (hydrolyze the ring = pharmacophore; amidases cleave the side chain but the ring remains intact); (2) efflux pumps (especially Gram-). Also target alteration (PBP). β-lactamase localization: Gram+ in the peptidoglycan, Gram- in the periplasmic space.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: β-lactamase inhibitors (see dedicated cards); enzyme classification according to Ambler (see β-lactamase card).

entries.name: Natural penicillins — Penicillin G (benzylpenicillin) / Penicillin V (phenoxymethylpenicillin)
entries.memo: Penicillin G, narrow spectrum; it remains the **gold standard for syphilis** even today.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam (transpeptidase inhibition); first β-lactams discovered; derivatives of 6-aminopenicillanic acid.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value:
PBP/transpeptidase.
Pharmacokinetic Parameters (Pen G): not hydrophilic → does not cross the porins of Gram-; fragile in gastric acidic environment (hydrolysis) → parenteral only, repeatedly; short half-life (30 min); renal elimination (filtration 10% + secretion 85%). Pen V: N/A pharmacokinetic details in the documents.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: spectrum restricted to Gram+ cocci and some aerobes/anaerobes: Streptococci, Staphylococcus (if not a β-lactamase producer), Neisseria meningitidis (crosses the inflamed meninges); syphilis (susceptible treponema, benzathine formulation).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: hypersensitivity (IgE-mediated → anaphylactic shock; delayed reactions/rash, serum sickness-like); GI discomfort; flora selection → pseudomembranous colitis; ↓ vitamin K → coagulation disorders (with warfarin).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: β-lactamases (S. aureus from ~90% susceptible in '46 to ~5% in '98); acid hydrolysis.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: probenecid (blocks the renal tubular transporter for acids → ↑ concentration/half-life); benzathine (depot form with low solubility → prolonged release, single administration, e.g. syphilis). Ambler: class A β-lactamases (penicillinases).

entries.name: β-lactamase-resistant penicillins (methicillin; isoxazolyl-penicillins: oxacillin, cloxacillin, dicloxacillin; nafcillin)
entries.memo: Bulky chain → they resist penicillinase (MSSA); **useless against MRSA** (altered PBP2a → vancomycin).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam; bulky side group → lower susceptibility to β-lactamases.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: isoxazolyl-penicillins → high plasma protein binding, renal elimination; not hydrophilic → do not cross the porins of Gram-. Nafcillin: biliary elimination.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: methicillin → originally S. aureus ("antistaphylococcal") and streptococci; isoxazolyl-penicillins → β-lactamase-producing Gram+ (respiratory tract, skin, soft tissues, genitourinary).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: methicillin → nephrotoxicity; isoxazolyl-penicillins → lower nephrotoxicity compared to methicillin.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) due to target alteration (PBP); methicillin is outdated today.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

entries.name: Aminopenicillins (amoxicillin, ampicillin)
entries.memo: Spectrum extended to Gram-; **+ clavulanate = Augmentin**; the **amoxicillin rash in mononucleosis is not an allergy**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam; addition of the amino group.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: hydrophilicity → they cross the porins → broad Gram+/Gram- spectrum; resistance to gastric acid (the side-chain nitrogen stabilizes the charge and protects the ring from hydrolysis) → oral route; low plasma protein binding; rapid renal elimination; good diffusion (CNS if meninges are inflamed, placenta). Time-dependent (maintain C > MIC for > 50% of the time); 3-4 administrations/day of 250-500 mg orally. Amoxicillin safe in pregnancy.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: infections of the airways, genitourinary, dermatological, soft tissues (non-β-lactamase-producing germs); E. coli, Proteus, Shigella; Neisseriae.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: hypersensitivity; GI discomfort (residual oral fraction in the GI tract → topical effect → flora selection → pseudomembranous colitis); ↓ vitamin K → coagulation (warfarin).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: susceptible to β-lactamases (ring opening).

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: amoxicillin + clavulanic acid (oral; ↑ oral availability vs ampicillin); ampicillin + sulbactam (also parenteral). Ambler: protection vs class A.

entries.name: Carboxypenicillins (carbenicillin, ticarcillin)
entries.memo: First **anti-Pseudomonas**, now superseded by the more potent ureidopenicillins.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam; "carboxy" substitution → spectrum broadening.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: broad spectrum; NOT resistant to gastric acid nor to β-lactamases → parenteral; renal elimination; half-life 1 h; time-dependent (ticarcillin); plasma protein binding ~45%.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: Gram+ (streptococci) and Gram- (Proteus, susceptible E. coli); infections of the airways, urogenital, dermatological, soft tissues.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: common to penicillins (hypersensitivity, GI discomfort); class-specific N/A in the provided documents.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: susceptible to β-lactamases.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

entries.name: Ureidopenicillins (piperacillin, mezlocillin, azlocillin)
entries.memo: **Piperacillin/tazobactam**: very broad spectrum, a mainstay of **empirical hospital therapy**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam; ureido group → ↑ hydrophilicity → selectivity toward Gram-.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: spectrum shifted toward Gram- (anti-Pseudomonas); low plasma protein binding; renal elimination; multiple administrations 3-4 g × 3/day (maintain C > MIC).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: difficult/nosocomial Gram- — Haemophilus influenzae, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa; pneumonias, intra-abdominal infections, skin, soft tissues, diabetic foot.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: hypersensitivity (dipeptide-like structure; IgE → anaphylaxis; delayed reactions/rash, serum sickness-like, severe cutaneous 0-5%); GI discomfort; ↓ vitamin K → coagulation.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: susceptible to β-lactamases.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: piperacillin + tazobactam (tazobactam = 1st-generation β-lactamase inhibitor). Ambler: protection vs class A.

entries.name: Cephalosporins (class — general overview)
entries.memo: Going up in generation: **↑ Gram- and CSF, ↓ Gram+** (the 4th recovers it); more stable to β-lactamases.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: identical to penicillins (same β-lactam ring → transpeptidation inhibition); molecule somewhat more resistant to β-lactamases. Structure: derivatives of 7-aminocephalosporanic acid, β-lactam ring fused to a 6-membered ring with sulfur (one more atom compared to penicillins).

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP/transpeptidase.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: classification into 4 generations by SPECTRUM (not by chronology): 1st → predominantly Gram+ → progression toward Gram- (3rd) → 4th re-broadens Gram+ and Gram-.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: per generation → see card.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: cross-hypersensitivity with penicillins (similar haptens → allergic to penicillins ≈ allergic to cephalosporins); hematocrit alteration with hemolysis; nephrotoxicity from tubular necrosis; disulfiram-like reaction ("sulfa" group → aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibition → acetaldehyde accumulation with alcohol → nausea, vomiting, incoordination); GI discomfort; interaction with oral anticoagulants.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: variable per generation (3rd resistant to β-lactamases).

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: combinations with β-lactamase inhibitors (e.g. ceftolozane+tazobactam; 3rd/2nd gen cephalosporins + avibactam/vaborbactam). Ambler: classes C/D hit the cephalosporins.

entries.name: 1st-generation cephalosporins (cefazolin, cephalexin)
entries.memo: **Cefazolin** = standard surgical prophylaxis (covers the Gram+ of the skin).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action / Target
segments.value: as the class (PBP).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: spectrum oriented toward Gram+ (some Gram- and anaerobes susceptible); half-life 1-2 h; important glomerular filtration; cephalexin orally (1-3 g × 4/day), cefazolin parenteral; multiple administrations.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: infections of the airways, otorhinolaryngological, soft tissues (susceptible germs).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: see class (cross-hypersensitivity, disulfiram-like, etc.).

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: no specifics N/A in the documents.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

entries.name: 2nd-generation cephalosporins (cefoxitin, cefamandole, cefuroxime, cefaclor, cefprozil)
entries.memo: More Gram-; **cefoxitin** is the exception that covers the **anaerobes** (abdominal infections).

segments.label: Mechanism of Action / Target
segments.value: as the class (PBP).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: spectrum broadened toward Gram- and anaerobes; cefuroxime parenteral (includes Haemophilus influenzae), cefaclor (pediatric syrup) and cefprozil orally; low t½; high glomerular filtration; multiple administrations 1-2 g several times/day.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: infections of the airways and soft tissues.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: see class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: Pseudomonas aeruginosa NOT susceptible.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: combinable with avibactam/vaborbactam (2nd gen inhibitors). Ambler: N/A specific for the generation.

entries.name: 3rd-generation cephalosporins (cefotaxime, ceftazidime, cefoperazone, ceftriaxone; oral: ceftibuten, cefpodoxime proxetil*)
entries.memo: **Ceftriaxone** passes into the CSF → **meningitis**; ceftazidime for Pseudomonas.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action / Target
segments.value: as the class (PBP).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: selective shift toward Gram-, includes Pseudomonas aeruginosa (especially ceftazidime, cefoperazone); resistant to β-lactamases; predominantly parenteral + multiple administrations. Ceftriaxone: ↑ lipophilicity + high plasma protein binding (>75%) → single administration/day (↑ compliance; the only one in single-dose among cephalosporins and penicillins); pain at the site → often + anesthetic.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: infections of the airways, soft tissues, genitourinary, immunocompromised patients, nosocomial, difficult germs.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: see class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: resistant to β-lactamases; further protection via avibactam/vaborbactam.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: 3rd gen + avibactam/vaborbactam; ceftolozane + tazobactam (new ceph similar to 3rd). Ambler: avibactam → classes A + C.

entries.name: 4th-generation cephalosporins (cefepime)
entries.memo: **Cefepime**: covers Gram+ and Gram-/Pseudomonas together → **neutropenic fever**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action / Target
segments.value: as the class (PBP).

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: very broad spectrum (Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae, Pseudomonas, Gram+ again); excellent distribution also in the CNS; low t½; 2 g × 2/day parenteral.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: moderate-severe infections of the airways, soft tissues, genitourinary, immunocompromised patients (bactericidal), nosocomial, difficult germs, peritonitis, biliary infections, meningitis.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: see class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: no specifics N/A in the documents.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents (for cefepime).

entries.name: New cephalosporins (ceftolozane, ceftaroline)
entries.memo: **Ceftaroline** = the only **anti-MRSA** cephalosporin; the combos with avibactam for MDR Gram-.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: see class.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: no specifics N/A in the documents.

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: ceftolozane + tazobactam.

freetext: Note: a "5th generation" is not defined (no significant alteration of the spectrum); the new molecules refer back to the existing generations.
Ceftolozane: parenteral; spectrum similar to the 3rd gen, anti-Pseudomonas activity; complicated intra-abdominal and genitourinary infections; in combination with tazobactam.
Ceftaroline: parenteral; spectrum similar to the 4th gen; activity against MRSA (methicillin-resistant S. aureus); complicated infections of skin and soft tissues.

entries.name: Carbapenems (imipenem, ertapenem, doripenem, meropenem)
entries.memo: Almost **universal** spectrum, last line of defense; **imipenem + cilastatin**; do not waste them or KPC/NDM arrive.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam (same mechanism); -COOH in the 5-membered heterocycle (simpler, without heteroatoms in the fused ring) → further resistance to β-lactamases.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: ULTRA broad spectrum; imipenem → short half-life, poor plasma protein binding, renal elimination; ertapenem → longer half-life; hospital use (not outpatient).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: infections of the genitourinary tract, bone, respiratory, skin and soft tissues; hospital setting.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: GI discomfort (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea); hypersensitivity; seizures in predisposed patients; important interactions with antiepileptics.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: INDUCERS of resistance (β-lactamases) → limit their use; vulnerable to carbapenemases (class A serine carbapenemases of Klebsiella; class B metallo-β-lactamases).

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: imipenem + cilastatin (cilastatin = inhibitor of the renal dipeptidases that metabolize the bond → ↑ persistence/half-life; kinetic adjuvant, NOT a β-lactamase inhibitor); meropenem + vaborbactam; imipenem + relebactam. Ambler: meropenem+vaborbactam active on carbapenemases (KPC) of Klebsiella.

entries.name: Monobactams (Aztreonam)
entries.memo: **Aztreonam**: only Gram- (Pseudomonas), **no cross-allergy** → useful in the penicillin-allergic patient.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: β-lactam with exclusive persistence of the β-lactam ring (maximum simplification, no fused ring); mechanism identical to the other β-lactams.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: PBP.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: selective spectrum toward Gram- (difficult germs); very useful vs Pseudomonas aeruginosa; parenteral every 6 h; inhalational use in cystic fibrosis.

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: infections of the genitourinary tract, respiratory, septicemias; cystic fibrosis (inhalational, for Pseudomonas/difficult Gram-).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: does NOT cause cross-hypersensitivity with other β-lactams (it lacks the responsible fused heterocycle); it can cause hypersensitivity due to different components; rarely pancytopenia, coagulation disorders, cutaneous alterations, seizures; (from slide) nausea, vomiting, diarrhea.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: resistant to metallo-β-lactamases (class B) — the metallo-β-lactamases are "the killer of all β-lactams except Aztreonam".

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

entries.name: β-lactamases — Ambler classification (resistance enzymes)
entries.memo: β-lactamases A/B/C/D; the **metallo (B: NDM/VIM)** use zinc and **no inhibitor stops them**.

segments.label: Definition
segments.value:
enzymes that hydrolyze the pharmacophore (β-lactam ring) of β-lactams.
Class A: the most common; they cleave extended- and narrow-spectrum penicillins (= penicillinases, class A of the first type); some derivatives also attack the cephalosporins. Subcategory: serine carbapenemases (act on carbapenems; the most concerning = Klebsiella pneumoniae / KPC, nosocomial infections).
Class B: metallo-β-lactamases — "killer of all β-lactams except Aztreonam".
Class C and D: lytic enzymes that recognize cephalosporins, oxacillin and congeners.
Localization: Gram+ in the peptidoglycan; Gram- in the periplasmic space.
Relevance: target of the β-lactamase inhibitors (see following cards).

entries.name: β-lactamase inhibitors — 1st generation (clavulanic acid, sulbactam, tazobactam)
entries.memo: **Clavulanate/sulbactam/tazobactam**: 'suicide decoys' that inactivate class A β-lactamases.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: structural analogues of β-lactams; they bind the active site of the β-lactamases and, once hydrolyzed, block it IRREVERSIBLY ("they sacrifice themselves" in place of the antibiotic); no antibiotic activity of their own (they do not act on the transpeptidases) → only in combination.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: active site of the β-lactamases.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value:
clavulanic acid active orally (tazobactam/sulbactam not orally, as per the lecture notes); the pharmacokinetics must be identical to that of the associated β-lactam (same Cmax/elimination/half-life) in order to reach the site of infection together.
Clinical Indications (inhibitory spectrum): active toward the penicillinases → Ambler class A (extended spectrum). No effect toward class A carbapenemases (Klebsiella), class B (metallo), class C, class D.

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: action only on class A; formulations in fixed combination (fixed ratio, no free choice of the partner).

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: amoxicillin + clavulanic acid (oral); ampicillin + sulbactam (parenteral); piperacillin + tazobactam.

entries.name: β-lactamase inhibitors — 2nd generation and new ones (avibactam, vaborbactam, relebactam; non-β-lactams: zidebactam, nacubactam)
entries.nameShort: β-lactamase inhibitors — 2nd generation and new ones
entries.memo: **Avibactam & co.**: they stop the KPC/OXA carbapenemases, **but not the metallo-β-lactamases (NDM/VIM)**.

segments.label: Mechanism of Action
segments.value: different molecular components (NOT β-lactam analogues); not hydrolyzed by the β-lactamases → inhibition without inactivation → "recyclable", longer-lasting action.

segments.label: Molecular Target
segments.value: class A β-lactamases (like the 1st gen) + class C; vaborbactam also Klebsiella carbapenemases (KPC). Zidebactam/nacubactam: also PBP (intrinsic antibiotic activity) → potential vs class B metallo-β-lactamases (not targetable today); non-β-lactam inhibitors also class D carbapenemases.

segments.label: Pharmacokinetic Parameters
segments.value: pairing with the β-lactam on a pharmacokinetic basis (similar elimination/half-life/distribution).

segments.label: Clinical Indications
segments.value: in combination with 3rd gen cephalosporins or meropenem (difficult/nosocomial settings; also 2nd gen cephalosporins). Meropenem + vaborbactam → complicated urinary tract infections (incl. pyelonephritis), intra-abdominal, ventilator-associated nosocomial pneumonias. Imipenem + relebactam (kinetic advantage).

segments.label: Common Adverse Reactions (ADR)
segments.value: N/A in the provided documents.

segments.label: Resistance / Efficacy Limits
segments.value: the fixed combinations do not guarantee efficacy for all the β-lactamases present (limit of the fixed combinations).

segments.label: Pharmacological Combinations / Adjuvants
segments.value: avibactam/vaborbactam + 3rd (or 2nd) gen cephalosporins / meropenem; relebactam + imipenem.
